title: The Neuroscience of Crying During Psychedelic Sessions and Why It Heals
body:
The Neuroscience of Crying During Psychedelic Sessions and Why It Heals

Have you ever found yourself in the middle of a psychedelic session, feeling overwhelmed by emotion, and suddenly burst into tears? You're not alone. Crying during psychedelic experiences is common, and honestly, it’s one of the most powerful signs that real healing is happening. But why does this happen? What’s going on in your brain when the tears start flowing? And how can crying actually help you heal? Let’s unpack this together.

Why Crying Feels So Natural on Psychedelics

First, let’s just admit something: crying is messy. It’s uncomfortable. It makes you vulnerable. But crying also feels natural. Your brain and body are telling you something needs to be released. Here’s the thing, psychedelics like psilocybin or LSD don’t just change your perception of the world around you. They shift your internal perception of yourself, your memories, and your emotions.

Scientists have shown that psychedelics reduce activity in the brain’s default mode network (DMN). This network is like the “autopilot” for your mind, the part that keeps your ego and self-identity intact. When it quiets down, you lose some of your usual mental filters. The tough memories, deep fears, or bottled-up emotions that you normally shove aside can surface. And suddenly, you might find yourself crying over something small or something huge, or even for no clear reason.

Does that land? You’re exposed, raw, and your brain’s telling you to let go.

The Brain Chemistry Behind Tears

There’s a real biological process behind crying, especially emotional crying. It’s not just about water coming out of your eyes. Crying triggers a cascade of neurochemical changes that help soothe your nervous system.

  • Oxytocin and Endorphins: When you cry, your brain releases oxytocin and endorphins, natural painkillers that reduce physical and emotional pain. It’s why you sometimes feel a little lighter after a good cry.
  • Cortisol Reduction: Crying can lower cortisol levels, the hormone linked to stress. Psychedelics already tend to reduce cortisol, so crying amplifies this calming effect.
  • Parasympathetic Activation: Tears activate your parasympathetic nervous system, the part responsible for rest and recovery. This helps your body transition from fight-or-flight to a state of calm - exactly what you need after an intense emotional release during a trip.

So, crying isn’t just an emotional outburst. It’s your brain’s way of healing itself on a chemical level.

What Happens in Your Brain During Crying on Psychedelics?

Neuroscientists have used brain imaging to study emotional crying. The amygdala, the seat of your brain’s fear and emotion center, lights up when you cry. Psychedelics also activate the amygdala but in a very different way than normal stress or anxiety. Instead of triggering panic, psychedelics make the amygdala more flexible, allowing you to revisit painful emotions without shutting down.

Imagine this: your brain is like a tightly closed fist holding onto grief, shame, or trauma. Psychedelics loosen the grip, and crying is the hand that opens the fist slowly but surely. This opening allows feelings you may have buried for years to come out safely.

Additionally, psychedelics increase connectivity between brain regions that don’t usually “talk” to each other. This means your rational brain and emotional brain can communicate more freely. You might suddenly understand why you feel a certain way, or connect past experiences to current emotions. That clarity can trigger tears, but it also starts a healing dialogue inside your head.

Why Crying Feels Like Healing

Crying is a release valve. When you’re on a psychedelic journey, you’re often peeling back layers of emotional buildup. Crying helps you move through these layers instead of getting stuck in them. That’s why many people say they feel lighter, freer, or more compassionate with themselves after a good cry on psychedelics.

Look, here’s something I hear all the time from people I work with: “I didn’t know I needed to cry until I did, and then it changed everything.” Right?! It’s because crying isn’t just about sadness. It’s about reclamation. You’re reclaiming your emotional health by facing what’s been hidden.

Truth is, this emotional release can also reset how your brain handles stress and trauma. After crying, your brain often rewires itself in a way that reduces the intensity of old emotional wounds. This rewiring is one reason why psychedelics are being studied as treatments for PTSD, depression, and anxiety.

Putting It Into Practice: What Should You Do When You Cry?

Crying during a psychedelic session can feel scary or embarrassing, especially if you’re used to holding your emotions in check. But here’s the advice I give:

  • Don’t fight it: If tears come, let them. Your body is doing exactly what it needs to do.
  • Allow vulnerability: You might feel exposed, but that’s part of the healing. You’re processing deep stuff.
  • Talk it out if you want: If you have a sitter or guide, let them know what you’re feeling. Sometimes just naming your emotions helps.
  • Journal afterward: Writing down what you experienced can help cement insights and emotional shifts.

If you want to learn more about how psychedelics can change your relationship with your mind, you might enjoy How to Change Your Mind by Michael Pollan (paid link). It’s a fantastic deep dive into the science and personal stories behind psychedelic healing.

The Role of Microdosing and Emotional Release

Microdosing psychedelics - taking very small doses - can also trigger emotional release, though usually less intense than full doses. Some people find that with microdosing, crying happens more subtly over days or weeks. It’s like the brain is gently loosening old emotional knots over time, instead of ripping them open all at once.

This slow emotional detox can still be powerful. If you’re curious about microdosing but want a grounded perspective, check out Stealing Fire (paid link). It talks about altered states and their impact on creativity and healing, including microdosing.

Stories from the Field: Real People, Real Tears

Let me share a quick story from one of the sessions I’ve witnessed. There was a woman named Maya who sat quietly for over an hour during her psilocybin journey. Suddenly, out of nowhere, she started crying. It wasn’t a sobbing meltdown but gentle tears that rolled down her cheeks. She said later it felt like she was finally mourning the loss of a friendship she’d been holding onto for years but never allowed herself to grieve.

That moment of crying released years of emotional tension locked in her body. She told me, “I feel like I can breathe again.” That’s why crying in these sessions matters. It’s not just an emotional mess; it’s a deep rebalancing.

How Crying Connects to Broader Healing

Crying during psychedelics often marks a shift toward self-compassion and acceptance. When you cry, you soften your inner critic. The brain areas tied to self-judgment quiet down, and those associated with empathy light up. That’s why after crying - even if it’s uncomfortable - you might feel more connected to yourself and others.

And this shift isn’t just incidental. Studies from places like Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies (MAPS) show that emotional breakthroughs, including crying, predict better outcomes in psychedelic therapy. The tears are often a sign that you’re moving past resistance and into healing territory.

What Science Still Needs to Figure Out

Despite all this, there’s still a lot we don’t fully understand. Why do some people cry buckets while others don’t shed a tear during similar sessions? How do different psychedelics affect crying and emotional release? What’s the exact sequence of brain events that lead from emotional buildup to tears?

Researchers at institutions like Nature Neuroscience are actively studying these questions. Meanwhile, if you want to explore consciousness and emotional healing from multiple angles, I highly recommend Waking Up by Sam Harris (paid link). It’s a thoughtful look at mindfulness and brain science that complements psychedelic research well.

How You Can Support Your Healing Journey

Crying is just one part of the process. To support your healing, consider integrating practices that help you stay grounded and compassionate with yourself. Breathwork, journaling, gentle movement, and supportive community all help. And if you’re growing your own relationship with psilocybin, maybe start with a mushroom growing kit (paid link) to ensure you have a trusted source.

If you want resources, tools, and personal coaching on this path, check out kalesh.love. I’m here to help you hold the space for your emotional breakthroughs.

And for more on emotional processing in psychedelic sessions, you can explore our article on processing emotions after psychedelic experiences. It’s a practical guide to what comes next once the tears dry.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
